request.question: Who was born earlier, Dallas Green or Brian Johnson?

title: Miniver Cheevy
Brian Johnson
Have It All (Bethel Music song)
Dallas Green (baseball)
Little Hell (album)
Richard B. Johnson
Dallas Green (musician)
Brian Johnson (pitcher)
Dallas Green Monarchs
Rose ave.
sentences: "Miniver Cheevy" is a narrative poem written by Edwin Arlington Robinson and first published in "The Town down the River" in 1910.

The poem, written in quatrains of iambic tetrameter for three lines, followed by a catalectic line of only three iambs, relates the story of a hopeless romantic who spends his days thinking about what might have been if only he had been born earlier in time.
Brian Francis Johnson (born 5 October 1947) is an English singer and songwriter.
In 1980, he became the third lead singer of the Australian rock band AC/DC, after the death of their second lead singer Bon Scott.
He and the rest of the band were inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2003.
In March 2016, Johnson stepped down from touring on the Rock or Bust World Tour due to hearing problems.

On 20 May 2016, Johnson stated in an interview "I've had a pretty good run" in AC/DC and implied that he might not be returning by saying "I'm just thankful, really, that I came out of it in one piece."
"Have It All" is a song by Bethel Music and Brian Johnson and is Bethel Music's lead and only single from their eighth live album, "Have It All" (2016).
The live version of "Have It All" was released on February 20, 2016 available on streaming, followed by the release of the studio version on May 6, 2016.
The song was written by Brian Johnson, Bobby Strand, Lindsey Strand, Mia Fieldes, and Joel Taylor, with production being handled by Chris Greely and Bobby Strand.
George Dallas Green (August 4, 1934 – March 22, 2017) was an American pitcher, manager and executive in Major League Baseball.
After playing for the Philadelphia Phillies, Washington Senators, and New York Mets from 1960 through 1967, he went on to manage the Phillies, New York Yankees, and Mets.

Green managed the Phillies when they won their first World Series title in 1980 over the Kansas City Royals, and as general manager of the Chicago Cubs from 1981 to 1987 he built the club which won a division title in 1984, the Cubs' first postseason appearance in 39 years.
Green had a losing record both as a pitcher and as a manager.
Nonetheless, in 1983 he was inducted into the Delaware Sports Museum and Hall of Fame.
He achieved notoriety for his blunt manner.
Little Hell is the third album by City and Colour, released on June 7, 2011.

Besides Dallas Green who recorded the majority of the instrumentals himself, contributing musicians on the record include Daniel Romano of Attack in Black, Dylan Green and Scott Remila of Raising the Fawn, Nick Skalkos of The Miniatures, Misha Bower of Bruce Peninsula and Anna Jarvis and Jordan Mitchell of The Rest.
The album art is an illustration of Allard Schager's photograph "Fields of Gold", taken in a tulip field outside Alkmaar in North Holland.
Richard Brian Johnson is the author of the book "Abominable Firebug" (ISBN  ), which presents his account of daily life at the Lyman School for Boys.
Johnson invented the Rubber Ducky antenna while attending the Lyman School for Boys.
Johnson went on to a career as an engineer and inventor.
He also created the JMODEM file transfer protocol.
Mr. Johnson founded the Danvers, Massachusetts, software company, Route 495 Software, LLC, in early 2009.
Johnson is also an activist for civil rights issues, and has communicated with the President of the United States on issues involving general aviation.
Johnson has continued to give talks to groups interested in learning about the nation's first reform school.
Dallas Michael John Albert Green (born September 29, 1980) is a Canadian musician and singer-songwriter who records under the alias City and Colour.
He plays melodic acoustic and folk music and is often accompanied by a rotating number of Canadian indie rock musicians, such as Spencer Burton.
He is also known for his contributions as the lead singer, rhythm guitarist, and songwriter for the post-hardcore band Alexisonfire.
In 2005, he debuted his first full-length album, "Sometimes", which achieved platinum certification in 2006.
City and Colour began performing in small intimate venues between Alexisonfire tours.
The name "City and Colour" comes from his own name: "Dallas", a city, and "Green", a colour.
His reasoning for the name was that he felt queasy "putting the album out under the name Dallas Green".
Christopher Brian Johnson (born December 7, 1990) is an American professional baseball pitcher for the Boston Red Sox of Major League Baseball (MLB).

Johnson was part of the No. 1 recruiting class by Baseball America at the University of Florida and was named a freshman All-American by "Baseball America", Yahoo! Sports, National Collegiate Baseball Writers Association, and Louisville Slugger in 2010.
Johnson also played for the collegiate national Team USA baseball squad in the summer of 2010.
Johnson was one of only two freshmen in the entire country, the other being Gator teammate Nolan Fontana, to play for Team USA.
The Dallas Green Monarchs were a semipro baseball team in the Negro Leagues from 1940-1947 and again in 1953.
The team was ostensibly the successor of the Dallas Black Giants which had disbanded after the 1938 season.
The 1940s team played the majority of their games at Rebel Field while the 1953 team played at Burnett Field.
From 1940-1942, their intracity "rival" was the Dallas Wonders who they played for the city tournament in 1940.
The Green Monarchs won the Dixie Negro Semipro tournament in 1947 and advanced to the National Semipro Congress.
Chicago Cubs' great Ernie Banks' was a bat boy for the Green Monarchs and his father Eddie Banks was its catcher.
Green Monarch Hank "Donkey" Thompson went on to Major League Baseball playing for the New York Giants.
The 1953 team played in the North Texas Negro Baseball League.
But the 1954 and 1955 Dallas team in that League was the Dallas Bluebirds and the Green Monarchs appear to have been dissolved.
rose ave. (also known as Rose Ave. or roseave.)
is the debut studio album by folk music duo You+Me, consisting of Dallas Green and Alecia Moore (Pink).
The album was released on October 14, 2014 through RCA and Dine Alone records.
All songs were written and produced by Pink and Green except "No Ordinary Love", a song by English band Sade.
The album debuted at number-one on the Canadian Albums Chart and at number-four on the "Billboard" 200.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: Who was born earlier, Dallas Green or Brian Johnson?
hop1_docs: Brian Johnson (footballer, born 1948) | Brian Joseph Johnson (born 29 October 1948) is an English footballer, who played as a midfielder in the Football League for Tranmere Rovers.

Brian Price (American football, born 1994) | Brian Keith Price (born June 24, 1994) is an American football defensive tackle for the Dallas Cowboys of the National Football League (NFL).  He played college football at Texas-San Antonio, and was signed by the Green Bay Packers as an undrafted free agent in 2016.

Brian Johnson (footballer, born 1955) | Brian Johnson (born 21 October 1955) is an English retired footballer who played as a winger.  He made 197 appearances in the Football League for Plymouth Argyle, scoring 40 goals, and a further seven while on loan at Torquay United.

Brian Johnson (ice hockey) | Brian Johnson (born April 1, 1960 in Montreal, Quebec) is a retired Canadian professional ice hockey right winger who played one season in the National Hockey League for the Detroit Red Wings.

Brian Johnson (fullback) | Brian "Horse" Johnson (born April 6, 1979) is a former American football fullback who played five seasons with the San Jose SaberCats of the Arena Football League (AFL).  He played college football at the University of New Mexico.
Brian Rogers (baseball) | Brian Alan Rogers (born July 17, 1982 in Dallas, Texas) is a former relief pitcher in Major League Baseball who played for the Pittsburgh Pirates in 2006 and 2007.
Brian Johnson (athlete) | Brian Johnson (born March 5, 1980 in Iowa, Louisiana) is an American long jumper.
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Brian Johnson (multiple individuals listed); Dallas Green is not present in the passages.

- "Brian Joseph Johnson" — "born 29 October 1948"; described as "English footballer" (midfielder).  
- "Brian Johnson" — "born 21 October 1955"; described as "English retired footballer" (winger).  
- "Brian Johnson (ice hockey)" — "born April 1, 1960"; described as "retired Canadian professional ice hockey right winger."  
- "Brian 'Horse' Johnson" — "born April 6, 1979"; described as "former American football fullback."  
- "Brian Johnson (athlete)" — "born March 5, 1980"; described as "American long jumper."

Ambiguities / missing facts:
- The passages list multiple distinct "Brian Johnson" individuals; which "Brian Johnson" the question intends is ambiguous.
- The passages do NOT include any "Brian Johnson" who is the AC/DC singer (no entry present) — birth date for that likely candidate is missing.
- "Dallas Green" does not appear in the provided passages; the target entity is therefore missing and ambiguous (possible candidates include Dallas Green the musician and Dallas Green the baseball manager).

Next-hop retrieval directives (priority order):
1. Fetch "Brian Johnson (singer)" or "Brian Johnson (AC/DC)" — inspect lede/infobox for exact birth date (to resolve which Brian Johnson is intended).  
2. Fetch "Dallas Green (disambiguation)" — then open "Dallas Green (musician)" lede/infobox and "Dallas Green (baseball)" (or "Dallas Green (manager)") lede/infobox to get Dallas Green's birth date(s).  
3. If the question instead refers to one of the Brian Johnsons already in the passages, open each relevant Brian Johnson page lede/infobox ("Brian Johnson (footballer, born 1948)", "Brian Johnson (born 1955)", "Brian Johnson (ice hockey)", "Brian Johnson (fullback)", "Brian Johnson (athlete)") to confirm birth dates for direct comparison.
response.hop2_query: Brian Johnson AC DC birth date
hop2_docs: Brian Johnson (disambiguation) | Brian Johnson (born 1947) is British lead singer of Australian hard rock band AC/DC.
Danger (AC/DC song) | "Danger" is a single by Australian rock band AC/DC, from the album "Fly on the Wall" released in 1985.  It was written by Brian Johnson, Angus Young, and Malcolm Young.

Fly on the Wall (AC/DC album) | Fly on the Wall is an album by Australian hard rock band AC/DC.  It was the band's ninth internationally released studio album and the tenth to be released in Australia.  All songs were written by Angus Young (guitar), Malcolm Young (guitar), and Brian Johnson (vocals).  The album was re-released in 2003 as part of the "AC/DC Remasters" series.

Brian Johnson | Brian Francis Johnson (born 5 October 1947) is an English singer and songwriter.  In 1980, he became the third lead singer of the Australian rock band AC/DC, after the death of their second lead singer Bon Scott.  He and the rest of the band were inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2003.  In March 2016, Johnson stepped down from touring on the Rock or Bust World Tour due to hearing problems.  On 20 May 2016, Johnson stated in an interview "I've had a pretty good run" in AC/DC and implied that he might not be returning by saying "I'm just thankful, really, that I came out of it in one piece."
Shake Your Foundations | "Shake Your Foundations" is a song and single by Australian hard rock band AC/DC, written by Angus and Malcolm Young and Brian Johnson.

Flick of the Switch | Flick of the Switch is the ninth studio album by Australian hard rock band AC/DC.  Released in 1983, it followed the highly successful "Back in Black" and "For Those About to Rock" albums.  "Flick of the Switch" is the third AC/DC album to feature lead vocalist Brian Johnson, and last to feature drummer Phil Rudd before his eleven-year break from the band.  It is the band's eighth internationally released studio album, the ninth to be released in their native Australia.  All songs on the album were composed by guitarists Malcolm and Angus Young and vocalist Johnson.

Anything Goes (AC/DC song) | "Anything Goes" is a song by the Australian hard rock group AC/DC.  It is the fourth track from their album "Black Ice".  "Anything Goes" is one of five songs from the album that were played live on their Black Ice World Tour, however it was removed from the setlist on 25 October 2009 and was not played for the remainder of the tour.  The single cover for Anything Goes is only the second AC/DC cover to feature frontman Brian Johnson alone (the 1986 re-release of "You Shook Me All Night Long" was the first); others have shown either the band or Angus Young.
response.summary_2: {"summary":"Brian Francis Johnson was born 5 October 1947. Dallas Green's birth date is not present in the provided passages, so a determination of who was born earlier cannot be made from these sources."}
response.answer: Cannot be determined from the provided summaries: Dallas Green's birth date is not given, and which Brian Johnson is intended is ambiguous.
